intro:
Water is one of Scripture's most pervasive images, holding together two seemingly opposite meanings: it is the indispensable source of life in the parched world of the Bible, and it is also the dark power of chaos and death. From these poles the whole biblical drama of judgment and salvation is told.

The story opens over water: the Spirit of God hovers over the formless deep before God divides the waters to make a habitable world (Genesis 1:2; Genesis 1:6–7). In the flood that same deep is unleashed as judgment, yet Noah is borne through it to a new beginning (Genesis 7:17–23). The pattern recurs at the Red Sea, where the waters drown Egypt but become Israel's path to freedom (Exodus 14:21–22) — a passage Paul calls a baptism (1 Corinthians 10:1–2). In the wilderness God gives water from the rock to a thirsting people (Exodus 17:6), and the law uses water to wash away defilement (Numbers 19:17–19).

The prophets deepen the image. To forsake the LORD is to abandon "the fountain of living waters" for broken cisterns (Jeremiah 2:13); restoration is pictured as a river flowing from the temple to heal the land (Ezekiel 47:1–12) and as a free invitation to the thirsty (Isaiah 55:1).

Jesus gathers every thread. He is baptized in the Jordan (Matthew 3:16), offers the woman at the well "living water" welling up to eternal life (John 4:14), and cries out that whoever believes will have rivers of living water flow from within — words John explains as the gift of the Spirit (John 7:38–39). From his pierced side flow blood and water (John 19:34). Believers are united to his death through baptism (Romans 6:3–4) and saved by "the washing of regeneration" (Titus 3:5).

Scripture closes where it began, by water — but now a crystal river of life flowing from God's throne through the city, with the Lamb's invitation: "Let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who desires take the water of life without price" (Revelation 22:1–2; Revelation 22:17). The chaotic sea is no more (Revelation 21:1); only the life-giving stream remains. Water thus traces the gospel itself: God brings life out of death, cleansing out of defilement, and his own indwelling Spirit to all who thirst for him.
